  • Portal Particle Acceleration In Jets

Astrophysical Jets

Astrophysical Jets

Type:Concept
Educational level:Undergraduate
Key Papers:Bromberg et al. (2011)

Astrophysical jets are collimated outflows launched from accreting compact objects or young stars. They transport energy and momentum over large distances and interact with the ambient medium, producing shocks and non-thermal emission.

Background

Jets appear across scales from protostars to microquasars to active galactic nuclei (AGN). Launching mechanisms include magneto-centrifugal disk winds and black-hole spin extraction (e.g., Blandford–Payne, Blandford–Znajek).

Physical description

  • Structure — A fast collimated core (jet) surrounded by a shocked cocoon formed by interaction with the ambient medium.
  • Collimation — External pressure from the cocoon or ambient medium can recollimate expanding jets, creating standing recollimation shocks.
  • Scale dependence — Jet power, velocity, and environment set the morphology and radiative signatures.

Why this matters

Jets are candidate accelerators of cosmic rays and producers of high-energy photons and neutrinos. Recollimation shocks in jets can be persistent acceleration sites; see [[recollimation-shocks]] and [[jet-cocoon-systems]].

Key references

  1. Bromberg et al. 2011 — analytic framework for jet–cocoon interactions (used and extended by 2603.16647).

Related concepts

  • Shock Waves
  • Jet–Cocoon systems
  • Relativistic jets

See also

  • Peretti et al. (2603.16647)
